reviews.comment: Sample review comment 1 for airflow_issue_10616_pr_10617_l_6bfba8c5. This is a test comment to validate the evaluation pipeline.
Sample review comment 2 for airflow_issue_10616_pr_10617_l_6bfba8c5. This is a test comment to validate the evaluation pipeline.
Sample review comment 1 for airflow_issue_12776_pr_12890_sm_db4e88c1. This is a test comment to validate the evaluation pipeline.
Sample review comment 2 for airflow_issue_12776_pr_12890_sm_db4e88c1. This is a test comment to validate the evaluation pipeline.
Sample review comment 3 for airflow_issue_12776_pr_12890_sm_db4e88c1. This is a test comment to validate the evaluation pipeline.

Deleting every non-"system" entry from `sys.modules` before each zip import is very risky: this can evict already-loaded third-party libraries or shared application modules that other parts of the scheduler/webserver still hold references to, leaving the process in a partially inconsistent state. It also changes behavior far beyond the packaged DAG being loaded. Can we narrow the cleanup to modules actually loaded from this zip (or from the DAG folder) instead of globally purging `sys.modules` based on `_is_system_module()`?

This branch removes any module that `_is_system_module()` does not recognize as platform-owned, but `_is_system_module()` relies on path-prefix heuristics and a small allowlist. That means site-packages installed outside the original `sys.path`, namespace packages, editable installs, or modules with unusual `__spec__.origin` values may be incorrectly deleted. Given the issue being fixed, this broad purge seems likely to introduce hard-to-debug regressions; consider tracking/restoring only modules imported during this load instead.

Using the import-time snapshot of `sys.path` as the definition of "system" can misclassify legitimate runtime-added import locations (plugins, virtualenv/sitecustomize changes, test harnesses, etc.) as non-system and cause their modules to be purged. Since this code runs in long-lived processes, the environment may evolve after module import. A safer approach would be to reason from the modules loaded during this zip import rather than from a stale global path snapshot.

This test calls `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_system_inner(...)`, but the test name/docstring describe the `external` scenario. That means the `AFTER` variant for the external case is never actually exercised, and this test duplicates coverage of the system-path case instead. Please call `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_inner(DependencyPosition.AFTER)` here.

This helper is missing a docstring opening/closing delimiter, so the following line is just a bare string expression rather than a function docstring. That makes the function body harder to read and is likely an accidental edit. Please wrap the description in triple quotes or remove it.

This migration is supposed to address the `dag_code.source_code` size limit, but the code here is altering `variable.val` instead. That looks inconsistent with both the issue and PR description, and would leave the original problem unresolved while changing an unrelated table. Please verify the target table/column and update the migration accordingly.

Downgrading `source_code` back to `TEXT` can fail or truncate data if any rows exceed the 64KB limit after this migration has been applied. It would be safer to either make the downgrade explicitly non-reversible for MySQL, or add a guard/check that prevents the alter when oversized values exist and raises a clear error message.

Switching from `UnicodeText` to generic `Text` changes the SQLAlchemy type semantics, not just the MySQL storage size. On several backends/dialects this can affect Unicode handling and, unless the migration explicitly uses a MySQL-specific type override, it may also alter the column type outside MySQL where the issue does not apply. Consider keeping the model as `UnicodeText` and handling the `MEDIUMTEXT` change in the MySQL migration/dialect-specific compilation instead, so we increase capacity without weakening cross-database Unicode guarantees.

This class-level import/version check makes the rule depend on importing the installed `airflow` package at module import time. That can make the upgrade-check tool fail or behave inconsistently in environments where `airflow` isn't importable yet, or where the package version differs from the code being executed. It would be safer to obtain the version through an existing utility/config path used by the upgrade checker, or defer this logic until runtime in a method where failures can be handled explicitly.

This changelog entry rewrites the historical example env var from `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD` to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD`. Since this is an old release note for #6801, changing it can make the changelog inaccurate for users looking up behavior in that version. Consider preserving the original text and, if needed, documenting the new section move in a new changelog or upgrade note instead.

This sentence is a bit unclear/grammatically off (`when reading new option`). Since this is upgrade guidance, it would help to explicitly say that Airflow now reads these settings from `[database]`, but will fall back to the legacy `[core]` keys with a deprecation warning for backward compatibility. That makes the migration path much easier to understand.

This switches the lookup to `[database]` only, which may break `airflow info` for users who still have `sql_alchemy_conn` configured under the legacy `[core]` section during the deprecation window. Since this PR is moving config keys rather than removing old ones outright, consider using the same compatibility path as the rest of the config system (e.g. a deprecated alias or an explicit fallback to `core`) so the command continues to report the DB URL correctly for existing installations.

The default value appears to have an accidental trailing `2` (`"utf-8"2`), which will make this YAML entry invalid and likely break config template generation/parsing. It should probably remain `"utf-8"`.

Since this change moves `sql_alchemy_conn` from `[core]` to `[database]`, please make sure backward compatibility is preserved for existing `airflow.cfg` files and environment variables. In particular, users may still have `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` set, so the config layer/docs should either keep reading the old key as a deprecated alias or clearly document the migration path; otherwise this default entry in the new section can be misleading because older deployments may silently stop picking up their configured DB URL.

This direct lookup now only checks the new `[database]` section. During the deprecation window, users may still have `sql_alchemy_conn` defined only in `[core]`, and other parts of this class already rely on `deprecated_options` to transparently resolve old locations. If `self.get()` here does not consult deprecated mappings in all call paths, this validation could fail or behave inconsistently for legacy configs. Consider using the same compatibility path explicitly (or adding a regression test) to ensure `[core] sql_alchemy_conn` still works until the deprecation is removed.

This example now only advertises the new `[database]` env var name. Since this change is a config-section migration, users upgrading existing pod templates may still be relying on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. If backward compatibility is still supported elsewhere in this PR, consider documenting that here (or keeping a note in the example comments/docs) so the example doesn't imply the old name stopped working immediately.

Switching the env var here is correct for the new section, but this template is user-facing and often copied verbatim. If backward compatibility still relies on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` being accepted, consider either documenting that this template now requires the new name or temporarily including both env vars to avoid breaking existing Kubernetes secrets/manifests during upgrades.

This direct section switch can break existing deployments that still define `sql_alchemy_schema` under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys, it would be safer to preserve backward compatibility here (for example, read from `[database]` first and fall back to `[core]`, ideally with a deprecation warning) until the old location is fully removed.

Reading only from `[database]` here may cause `sql_engine_collation_for_ids` to silently stop working for users who haven't migrated their config yet. Consider keeping a fallback to the old `[core]` section during the deprecation window so upgrades remain non-breaking.

This change removes compatibility with the legacy `[core] sql_alchemy_conn` setting. Because this value is used to infer MySQL/MariaDB-specific defaults, falling back to the old section is important to avoid behavior changes during upgrade for installations that haven't moved their config yet.

This switches the primary DB connection lookup to `[database]` only. Since this is a config-section migration, removing the old `[core]` lookup here can break existing installations unless `conf.get()` already transparently maps deprecated section/option names. Please confirm there is a compatibility layer for `core.sql_alchemy_conn` (including env var aliases like `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`) or add an explicit fallback/deprecation path here.

Same backward-compatibility concern applies to imported connect args: this now ignores the legacy `[core] sql_alchemy_connect_args` unless the config layer remaps deprecated keys. If that remapping is not already in place, consider checking the old location as a fallback and emitting a deprecation warning.

This reads `sql_alchemy_engine_args` from `[database]`, but that option is not listed in the issue/PR description among the settings being moved. If the templates/docs/deprecation mapping were only updated for the enumerated options, this change will create an undocumented config move. Please either keep this under `[core]` for now or make sure the new section, docs, and compatibility aliases include `sql_alchemy_engine_args` as well.

This switches `load_default_connections` to the new `[database]` section, but `initdb()` is part of the upgrade path and may run against existing installations that still only have `[core] load_default_connections` configured. Unless `conf.getboolean()` already provides a deprecation fallback for moved options, this can silently change behavior and skip creating default connections after upgrade. Please ensure this read goes through a compatibility layer (or explicitly falls back to `[core]`) until the old key is fully deprecated.

This switches the lookup to `[database]`, but it also drops compatibility with existing configs that still define `max_db_retries` under `[core]`. Since this value is read at import time, older installations may silently fall back to `3` instead of honoring the user’s configured value. Please consider using the deprecation-aware config access (or an explicit fallback to `core.max_db_retries`) so the migration preserves existing behavior.

This direct section switch can break compatibility for environments that still have `sql_alchemy_conn` only under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys between sections, it would be safer to read via the same deprecation/alias mechanism used elsewhere (or fall back to `[core]`) so imports of this module don't fail before users migrate their config.

Similar to the change above, this now assumes `sql_alchemy_conn` exists in `[database]` immediately. If backward compatibility is expected for old configs, consider using a helper that honors deprecated locations or falls back to `[core]`; otherwise JSON support checks may start raising config errors during startup.

This direct section switch can break existing deployments that still define `sql_alchemy_conn` under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys rather than introducing a brand-new setting, it would be safer to preserve backward compatibility here by reading `[database]` first and falling back to `[core]` (or using the config deprecation/renamed-option mechanism if one exists elsewhere in the codebase). Otherwise the web app may fail to start until users manually migrate their config.

Switching the built-in secret env var gate from `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` to only `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` can break existing Helm values files during upgrades. Users who still set `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` will silently stop getting the metadata DB env var injected. Consider supporting both keys here for a deprecation period (e.g. `or` the old and new flags) to preserve backward compatibility while the config section migration rolls out.

Renaming this schema key to only `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` may break existing Helm values that still use `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. Since this PR is moving config sections, it would be safer to keep the old key as a deprecated alias (or otherwise document and handle the migration) so chart users don't lose the env var injection unexpectedly during upgrade.

Switching the built-in secret env var to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is correct for the new section, but this is a breaking change for existing Helm values that override `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. Unless the chart has compatibility logic elsewhere, those user overrides will silently stop applying after upgrade. Consider keeping the old key as a deprecated alias for one release or documenting this explicitly in the chart upgrade notes.

This update changes the documented env var to the new `[database]` section, but users upgrading may still rely on the legacy `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` alias for backward compatibility. If the code still supports the old name, it would be helpful to mention that here as a deprecated/legacy alternative to avoid making the docs look like the old variable stopped working immediately.

There is a typo in the environment variable name here: `AIRFLOW__DATABE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is missing the `AS` in `DATABASE`. As written, users copying this value will end up with a non-functional configuration. Please change it to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`.

The section name in this CLI example appears to be misspelled as `databse`. This should match the new config section name `database`, otherwise the documented command will fail for users trying to verify the setting.

This example now only shows the new `[database]` env var name. Since this PR is moving a long-standing config key, it would be helpful to mention whether `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is still accepted as a deprecated alias for backward compatibility. Otherwise readers upgrading existing deployments may assume the old variable stops working immediately and miss the migration path.

This updates the example to the new `[database]` section, but it also changes the env var name used by existing docker-compose setups. Since this file is a copy-paste entry point for many users, consider whether we should keep a temporary comment or compatibility note nearby (or in the surrounding docs) explaining that `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` was renamed, otherwise upgrades may fail silently for users following older examples.

This updates the env var reference to the new `[database]` section, but users upgrading from older versions may still rely on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. If backward compatibility or deprecation support still exists, it would be helpful to mention the old variable here as an alias/deprecated name to avoid confusion during upgrades.

This update reflects the new `[database]` section, but the Helm chart may still support the legacy `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` env var for backward compatibility during upgrades. If that fallback still exists, documenting only the new variable could mislead users migrating existing deployments. Consider mentioning both variables here, or explicitly noting that the old one is no longer supported by the chart.

This updates the env var to the new `[database]` section, but please double-check backward-compatibility in the config loader for CI/bootstrap paths. If any scripts or images used by this compose file still read only `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`, this change could break MySQL-based CI before the deprecation alias is applied. It may be worth confirming that both section names are accepted during the transition or documenting why this file is safe to switch now.

This expectation was updated to the new `[database]` section, but the chart still exposes `AIRFLOW_CONN_AIRFLOW_DB` on the next line. Since Airflow supports both the config key and the connection-based override for the metadata DB, it would be good to verify that the Helm chart templates and any built-in secret env var mappings were updated consistently so we don't end up with mixed `CORE`/`DATABASE` names or duplicate/inconsistent behavior during upgrades.

These tests now only exercise the new `[database]` location. Since this change is a config-section migration and Airflow typically preserves backward compatibility for renamed/moved options, it would be good to add coverage that `sql_alchemy_conn` is still resolved correctly from the legacy `[core]` section as well (or explicitly assert that the old location is no longer supported). Otherwise we may miss a regression in the deprecation/alias path.

This changes the DAG-level `latest` restriction to ignore task `end_date`s unless *all* tasks define one, which fixes the reported bug but also changes behavior for mixed DAGs in a way that may be too broad. In particular, a single task without `end_date` now makes every other task's `end_date` irrelevant to DAG scheduling. Please add/verify tests for mixed cases (some tasks bounded, some unbounded, with and without `dag.end_date`) to ensure this matches the intended scheduler semantics and doesn't regress existing expectations.

The `len(end_dates) == len(self.tasks)` check is a bit fragile and the inline comment is hard to read. It also relies on `self.tasks` not changing between the list comprehension and this comparison. Consider expressing the intent directly with something like `all(t.end_date is not None for t in self.tasks)` and updating the comment/docstring to clarify why DAG `latest` should only be derived from task end dates when every task is bounded.

This changes the task eligibility check for normal scheduled runs, but `self.is_backfill` still bypasses both `start_date` and `end_date`. That means backfill runs can still create task instances outside a task's declared `end_date`, which seems inconsistent with the issue being fixed. If task-level `end_date` is meant to be respected universally, consider applying the `end_date` constraint to backfills as well, or add a test/documentation clarifying that backfill intentionally ignores it.

This test only covers cases where all task-level `end_date`s are either bounded before the DAG restriction or one task is unbounded. Since the bug is specifically about a single task-level `end_date` incorrectly stopping the whole DAG, it would be valuable to add an assertion for the mixed case from the issue report (e.g. one task with `end_date`, another without) and verify that `dag._time_restriction.latest` remains `None` when the DAG itself has no `end_date`. Without that scenario, the regression described in the issue is not directly exercised.

Updating the backfill expectation from 2 to 3 changes the test semantics significantly: for a backfill run at `DEFAULT_DATE`, a task whose `end_date` is `DEFAULT_DATE - 1 day` would normally be out of range and should not get a TI. If the production fix is intended to stop task-level `end_date` from truncating the whole DAG, this test should also assert that only the unaffected tasks are created, rather than expecting all three tasks for backfill. Otherwise the test may encode behavior that ignores `end_date` filtering entirely.

Using `timezone.utcnow()` here makes the test time-dependent and can introduce flakiness, especially if `create_task_instance` or related assertions compare `execution_date`/logical date against a separately-evaluated `utcnow()`. Since this is a unit test, it would be safer to derive both values from a single fixed timestamp (or capture `now = timezone.utcnow()` once and reuse it) so the test remains deterministic while still avoiding the old `DEFAULT_DATE` issue.

This change replaces a fixed test date with a dynamic current time, which can make the test non-deterministic. If the goal is only to ensure `end_date` is after the task instance's date, consider capturing a single `now` value and using it consistently throughout the test, or using a fixed aware datetime, to avoid subtle timing-related failures.

Switching to `timezone.utcnow()` here may hide regressions behind wall-clock behavior and make the test harder to reproduce. A deterministic timestamp (or a single shared `now` captured once) would keep the test stable while still ensuring `end_date` is in the future relative to the task instance.

Passing `self.extra_args` through to `Object.load()` can break existing callers because `extra_args` on `S3Hook` is also used for transfer/download options, and not all of those keys are valid for `HeadObject`. For example, options like `ServerSideEncryption` or `StorageClass` are accepted by upload/copy APIs but will cause parameter validation errors here. It would be safer to filter `self.extra_args` to only the subset supported by `head_object`/`load()` (e.g. SSE-C, RequestPayer, ExpectedBucketOwner, VersionId, etc.) before forwarding them.

Converting any aware datetime to UTC here fixes the reported bug, but it also silently broadens the trigger's accepted input contract. Previously this constructor rejected non-Pendulum/UTC tzinfo values; now it will accept any aware datetime that `convert_to_utc` can handle. Please add or update tests to cover at least: naive datetimes still raising, non-UTC aware datetimes being normalized correctly, and existing UTC/Pendulum inputs preserving behavior. That will help guard against regressions in trigger serialization/deserialization and async sensor behavior.

This assertion hard-codes the expected UTC wall-clock time (`01:00`) and only passes if `DEFAULT_TIMEZONE` is a fixed UTC+8 offset. The issue description mentions DAG-specific timezones (e.g. `Europe/Berlin`), so this test should derive the expected value from the DAG's timezone instead of relying on a global default. Otherwise the test can give false confidence and won't catch regressions for non-UTC+8 DAGs or DST-aware zones.

Adding `QUEUED` to the event buffer changes the contract of `BaseExecutor`: scheduler code consuming `get_event_buffer()` has historically handled terminal states (and in some cases `RUNNING`) from executors. Before introducing this helper, please verify all event-buffer consumers correctly process `TaskInstanceState.QUEUED` and won't regress by re-queuing or overwriting state unexpectedly during task adoption.

This lookup assumes every described ECS task ARN maps back to exactly one `TaskInstance` in `tis`, but `__describe_tasks()` can return a subset and may also omit/reshape entries for invalid or no-longer-running ARNs. Using `[...][0]` here will raise `IndexError` if the response contains an unexpected task or if the ARN matching fails, which would abort adoption for the whole batch. Consider building a dict keyed by `external_executor_id` and handling missing matches defensively.

Only the `tasks` portion of `describe_tasks` is processed here. ECS also returns a `failures` list for ARNs that could not be described (for example, tasks that have already stopped or no longer exist). Those TIs will silently fall through to `not_adopted_tis`, but without any logging/metrics it's hard to distinguish expected non-adoption from API issues. It would be helpful to inspect and log `failures` so operators can understand why adoption did not happen.

For newly launched tasks, `add_task()` is called with `attempt_number`, but adoption uses `ti.prev_attempted_tries`. Can you confirm these values have the same semantics? If `prev_attempted_tries` is off by one relative to the executor's internal attempt counter, adopted tasks may be tracked with a different retry/attempt number than tasks started by this executor instance.

This assertion is a bit too weak for the adoption behavior being added. `assert_called_once()` only verifies that `describe_tasks` was invoked, but not that it was called with the expected task ARNs (and ideally only the non-`None` `external_executor_id`s). If the implementation accidentally queried the wrong IDs or included `None`, this test would still pass. Consider asserting the exact call arguments to make the test validate the adoption contract more precisely.
